reviews.review_text:
Performance can be rough from patch to patch.

Cosmetics are overpriced.

But there is no better horde shooter out there. 40k/10

I love this game.

I love the setting. I love the atmosphere. I love the combat. When Darktide works, it is one of the most satisfying horde shooters I have ever played. The frantic gameplay, the overwhelming enemy waves, the constant pressure, the unfairness of the situation — all of that fits Warhammer 40k perfectly. It is miserable, brutal, chaotic, and that is part of the charm.

It also does something very few games do for me: it calms my ADHD brain. The moment-to-moment combat is so intense and demanding that I can completely switch off. I genuinely love that.

But the higher the difficulty gets, the more the game starts falling apart.

Darktide throws huge masses of enemies at you, especially on Auric and high-intensity missions. That is not the problem. In fact, that is exactly what I want from this game. The problem is that this kind of design requires extremely reliable enemy behavior, hit detection, audio cues, and rules of engagement. When the game is this chaotic, the framework itself needs to be bulletproof.

And sadly, it is not.

The two biggest issues for me are broken enemy behavior and unreliable audio cues.

Special enemies are supposed to be announced through sound. Trappers, Poxbursters, Mutants, Hounds — they all have distinct audio cues so you can react before they reach you. On lower difficulties, this mostly works. There are fewer enemies, fewer threats, and enough space between specialists that the audio cues are usually clear.

On higher difficulties, especially Auric high-intensity missions, this system becomes unreliable. When the screen is full of enemies and multiple specialists spawn at once, the audio cues often do not match what is actually happening. You may hear two Mutants, but five are charging. You may not hear a Poxburster until it is already on top of you. You may not get a proper warning for a Trapper before you are already disabled.

That is a huge problem in a game where you rely heavily on sound to survive.

When I die because I failed to react properly, that is fine. When I die because the game did not give me the information it is supposed to give me, that is frustrating. On high difficulties, that difference matters a lot.

The second major issue is enemy behavior.

Hounds can behave in ways that feel completely wrong. Sometimes they jump backwards, sideways or in ways that do not visually make sense. You dodge them, but they still seem to snap onto you from an illogical angle.

Trappers are even worse. They can trap you through railings, stairs, ledges, and geometry that should clearly block them. They can pull you through staircases, from above or below, or through obstacles that you as a player cannot interact through in the same way. In a normal mission this is annoying. In an Auric high-intensity mission, where you are already fighting through a full screen of enemies, it becomes genuinely infuriating.

Then there are Poxbursters.

Poxbursters are walking bombs, and their basic mechanic is fine: shoot them, stagger them, shove them away, or dodge them. The problem is that their behavior is not consistent enough. Once they lock onto a player and start their jump, it often feels like other players cannot reliably push them away, even if they are right there and facing them. Sometimes this makes the hit feel unavoidable.

Even worse, they can cut corners mid-jump and still land directly in your face. Combined with the very sharp explosion radius and damage falloff, a tiny positioning difference can mean either taking no damage or losing most of your health. On high difficulties, where you are constantly dodging, pushing, blocking, shooting, and reacting to several threats at once, that margin of error becomes extremely frustrating.

Now combine all of that:

Missing or inconsistent audio cues.
Specialists spawning in large numbers.
Enemies grabbing or hitting you through geometry.
Poxbursters bending around corners.
Dogs snapping onto you from weird angles.
A full horde blocking your vision.
Auric-level damage and pressure.

At that point, the game starts feeling less like a test of skill and more like a lottery. Sometimes you lose because you made a mistake. That is fine. But too often, you lose because the game’s systems did not behave reliably.

That is what makes me rage quit. Not the difficulty itself. I like the difficulty. I like the unfairness of the world. I like being overwhelmed. That is Darktide at its best.

But unfair gameplay only works when the rules are consistent.

Until the audio cue system and specialist enemy behavior are fixed, I cannot recommend Darktide, even though I love it and will probably keep playing it. The core game is fantastic, but the frustration on higher difficulties becomes overwhelming.

Darktide is an amazing game buried under issues that matter most exactly where the game should shine: at high-level play.

EDIT: 

Just to clarify where I’m coming from:

I love this game. I will continue playing it. I am honestly not even much of a gamer in general — Darktide is basically the only game I regularly play, and I have more hours in it than my Steam profile even properly reflects.

But for me, the review question is not “Do I personally still play this game?” The question is: “Would I recommend this game to a friend?”

And right now, even though I love Darktide, I would not recommend it without serious caveats.

The reason is simple: to really appreciate what Darktide is trying to do, the game needs to be consistent. Any game falls apart the moment its own rules stop behaving reliably. If you play chess and someone suddenly makes a move outside the ruleset, the entire game immediately becomes frustrating. It is no longer about losing fairly inside the game’s logic. It becomes frustration with the framework itself.

That is the issue I have with Darktide.

I actually like the overwhelming odds. I like the pressure. I like the unfairness of the 40k setting. I like being swarmed by waves of enemies while trying to survive with a good squad. I even like losing when the team gets overwhelmed after a good fight. That feeling is part of what keeps me coming back.

But that kind of frustration only works when the rules are consistent.

Fatshark is trying to create a game that is stressful, chaotic, and punishing by design. I respect that. I also understand that this is not an easy thing to pull off. But it also means the bar for consistency is extremely high.

My problem is that Darktide often fails exactly when it should shine the most.

On lower difficulties, many of these problems are annoying but manageable. On Auric, high-intensity, and similar difficulty modifiers, the margins become so narrow that unreliable audio cues, broken enemy behavior, strange specialist interactions, and inconsistent geometry interactions become much more than minor bugs. They start deciding runs.

And that takes away the feeling I actually want from the game.

I want to lose because the enemy pressure was too much. I want to lose because we made mistakes. I want to lose because the squad got overwhelmed in a brutal but fair fight.

I do not want to lose because a trapper pulled me through geometry, a poxburster curved around a corner, a dog snapped onto me from a weird angle, or an audio cue failed to play when the entire design of the game expects me to react to that cue.

That is why my review is negative.

Not because I hate the game. Quite the opposite. Darktide is one of the only games I genuinely keep coming back to. It could honestly be close to perfect for me. But the frustrating part is that the same high-difficulty chaos that makes the game amazing also exposes the inconsistent systems that make it fall apart.

So yes, I love Darktide. I play Auric and high-intensity missions. I will continue playing it.

But would I recommend it to a friend in its current state?

No. Not without warning them first.

I would give this game a mixed review but i cant...

In true fatshark fashion, the game went from "Don't buy" at launch, to "pretty decent" 1-2 years later, to "one of the best Coop games I ever played". 
If you're into 40k and action/coop games this game is for you. If you like crafting different builds on a bunch of different character-classes who all have unique skill-trees and a variety of different weapons with blessings (weapon enchantments) this game is for you.

[b] Positives  [/b]

- Satisfying gameplay: The guns feel amazing. The melee combat is a dream. Most weapons have different versions of themselves (called "Marks") with varied movesets. At surface level with most weapons you can light attack, heavy attack, special attack, push attack, and sometimes sprint attack. All of these combat maneuvers can be interwoven and mixed up and reward players learning the specific moveset of their chosen weapon. In addition you can block, dodge, slide, jump, sprint and vault over smaller obstacles. Mastering movement and positioning becomes a crucial skill if you choose to play on higher difficulties. If you don't want to learn the intricacies of the combat system and just want to crush heretic skulls with a Thunder Hammer, you can do that too. There are lots of difficulty settings, it doesn't have to be a difficult game if you don't want it to be.

- Fatshark supports their games for a long time. Over the years lots of new content was added (both paid and free) and the recent developer notes about the patch (May 11, 2026) make it clear to me, that they care about the longevity of this game. They are actively trying to reign in some of the powercreep which seemingly always happens over the years in these kind of games when new content is introduced.

[b] Negatives / Critical points to keep in mind before buying: [/b]

- The game is quite CPU heavy and doesn't like AMD GPUs! There have been numerous reports of graphic- and framerate-issues with AMD cards over the years. Be sure to do some research before buying! If you have solid CPU (Intel or Ryzen both worked fine for me) and a semi old NVIDIA GPU (I still run a GTX 1660) you'll be fine.

- Microtransactions for skins. This isn't inherently bad but the microtransactions in this game are predatory imo. First: you can't buy them directly. You can only buy a special currency in predetermined amounts, trough which you can then buy the skin (obfuscating prices and generating "leftover, wasted" currency which is of course by design so you want to buy more afterwards... otherwise it'll go to waste...right??? - Press F to spit.) Second: The pricing is ridiculous and they blatantly use FOMO mechanics to psychologically manipulate susceptible people into buying their desired skin NOW. There is a "rotation" of skins in the shop creating artificial scarcity and it is undisclosed when and if a certain skin will reappear for purchase again.

[b] Conclusion: [/b]

One of the best coop action games there is. I'm biased though because I also love their other popular coop game "Vermintide 2", which is quite similar. 
BUT... avoid the "Commodores Vestures" microtransactions store like the plague (unless you're a paypiggy and get off on being abused)
Good game and some greatly needed balance changes just went through to mix up the meta. If you like 40k and horde shooters this is the game for you. Just remember to be kind and respectful of others.

i got this in early access...for more than current base price. no imperial edition either.
since then we have gotten:
0 new cosmetics buyable without real money
0 promises fulfiled, including a backtrack on free classes and solo mode
20+ premium cosmetic sets that cost over $12 EACH
and
$12 class dlcs with seperate cosmetic dlcs
meanwhile the main gameplay has barely improved since.
Fatshark are traitors to their fanbase. do not buy this, even on sale. let nugle have atoma.

Warhammer 40,000: Darktide is a solid game, especially if you get it on sale. The combat feels heavy and satisfying, the atmosphere is great, and the visuals and sound design perfectly fit the Warhammer 40K setting.

The game does get repetitive after a while because the missions don’t change much, but the gameplay is still fun, especially with friends. I wouldn’t pay full price for it, but for a discounted Steam price, it’s completely worth it if you like co-op horde shooters.

Don't get me wrong here, the game really is fun, but it doesn't really seem like fatshark is all that invested in working on anything meaningful except for new DLC characters. They haven't added earnable cosmetics in forever, and at time of writing, they are gearing up to release the third DLC character. In the time that they have added all three, they have only added one new game mode. Again, the game is fun, but its extremely repetitive, and additions to the core of the game itself and earnable things takes a constant back seat to the payed shop and DLC that you have to buy. I get that they need to make money, but the core experience should be left to stagnate in that pursuit.

The core of the game is pure FUN! But for the life of me, I can't get a stable FPS. It fluctuates wildly, jumping anywhere from 20 to 100 FPS, with an average range of 20 to 47. I've tried playing on low and high settings, and I don't use ray tracing. Even when the FPS is on the higher side, the game still stutters! I've tried following optimization guides and reinstalling the game. I really want to recommend this game I really do because it's incredibly fun when it actually works.

So much fun but runs so bad.
Looks great but not so great that I'm getting such terrible performance on such powerful hardware. Constant fps drops, I cant get a stable framerate over 60fps on the third most powerful GPU on the market. Its been 4 years and I'm getting the same performance I did 4 years ago on my 6950XT. Please optimize the game.

This game fill in a niche that is not for everyone, but if you are interested in a melee horde game this is the best out there.

Music 10/10
Gameplay 10/10
Graphics: 8/10
Story 2/10
Perforamnce 6/10

The game is genuinely fun but I cannot fathom how bad the optimization is, I upgraded from my I5 11400h to a ryzen 9 6900hx and I genuinely got like 10 fps more, FATSHARK PLEASE, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ FIX YOUR GAME, SOME PEOPLE GENUINELY DO NOT HAVE THE ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ OPTION OF HAVING A 5000$ COMPUTER TO PLAY YOUR ♥♥♥♥♥♥ GAME

Early into my time with this game, but I can tell it's got a high ceiling for co-op shooter fun. Are the cosmetics and DLC price tags a little lame? Yeah, but they're not mandatory. I got it on a deep sale, I'm fine with throwing a lil bread on a new class that's also on sale. The game is very fun.

This is a great team-based Horde-style game, but it needs polishing. Constant crashes and performance issues prevent me from fully experiencing the otherwise satisfying gaming experience. I hope the developer takes action so I can change my review. For now, I can't recommend Ps Soundtrack. It's simply on another level. Thank you so much, master Jesper Kyd.

After 1000+ hours, I cannot recommend this game, which pains me to say. 

Fatshark has been consistently downgrading the game, making balance changes that make no sense, and there are multiple talents and perks in the game that have been and still are broken that aren't even on their "known issues" list despite people mentioning them constantly. All of that on top of a heavy monetization framework (paying for the 2 strongest classes in the game - which they keep nerfing - and very overpriced cosmetics) and a mentality of "nerf meta things, never buff unusable weapons", has completely ruined the game over the course of these last few years. 

An example of this is the most recent patch aimed at "addressing armor penetration meta". Starting by adding a flat 35% reduction in spawns of the beefiest, most heavily armored enemy in the game, and then taking the weapons that were able to deal with armor really well and gutting their blessings (think perks) that enabled them to actually do decent damage to armored targets. Rather than taking weapons that had no viability against armor and giving them blessings to help with it, or increasing their values against armor, they just took away the few tools we had at our disposal - their logic being that its fine because they lowered armored crusher spawns across the board. So rather than have an enemy type in the game and let us figure out how to deal with it, you're just going to remove the enemy 35% of the time (after previously doubling their health pool in another patch). How does that make the game more fun to play? 

All this patch is going to accomplish, as so many other patches have done before, is make the meta weapons still be meta albeit worse versions of themselves. You still can't bring a loadout that has no ability to deal with armored enemies because they still spawn, and there's plenty of weapons in the game that have no answer to armor. 

This latest patch is just the most recent iteration of Fatshark only listening to the 1% of their playerbase that screams how everything is broken and the game is too easy - the same 1% that use mods that automate the game for them, and have like 10K+ hours in the game. At the same time, the game has been bleeding players and currently has a smaller playerbase than Vermintide 2. 

Even if you're a big 40K fan, I cannot in good faith recommend you get into this game, and especially cannot recommend you spend any money buying the DLC classes or cosmetics. It's clear that Fatshark doesn't actually play their own game (especially not on Auric Maelstrom or Havoc 40 - the two hardest difficulties in the game) and they're completely content just getting what money they can out of players before they inevitably quit. 

I wish I hadn't given this game so much of my time, but the gameplay itself was really something, and all the movement and weapon tech you could learn made the original skill ceiling very high, which was appealing to me. Unfortunately they've just repeatedly made poor design and balance choices and have ruined what could have otherwise been a great game.
It's pretty boring at first but harder content is when the game starts getting really interesting, however it desperately needs more content and better optimization.
Unlike Vermintide 1 & 2, this game does not allow a true solo mode for when your friends are unavailable. It's public games or at least 2 players in "private" games.

[h1]Warhammer 40,000: Darktide — Worth It?[/h1]

[h2]DLC Policy[/h2]
My main concern with the game is its DLC policy. The base game costs €40, and with the upcoming third class DLC, the three classes together cost €36 — almost the same as the base game itself. Paywalling core content like new classes is unacceptable, especially since the game already has microtransactions in the form of purchasable Aquila credits and cosmetics that cannot be earned through gameplay.

[h2]Performance[/h2]
The game also has performance issues, which others have elaborated on in their reviews. I personally experience occasional lag and FPS drops that my current specs shouldn't cause.

[h2]The Good[/h2]
On the other hand, the game has real potential and offers a far better co-op and teamwork experience than Space Marine 2, with noticeably tighter controls. (I also wrote a review about that game, if you're interested.)

[h2]No Rewards for Failed Missions[/h2]
One issue I feel strongly about — and it applies to both Darktide and Space Marine 2 — is the complete lack of rewards for failed or abandoned missions, regardless of the reason. It doesn't matter how far you got, how many enemies you killed, how many resources you collected, or whether the failure was even your fault. A disconnect, a game-breaking bug wipes your progress clean.

In one mission I collected over 360 plasteel, cleared multiple checkpoints, and fought through endless enemy waves — only to be forced to abandon due to a known bug in the servo skull scanning objective. I walked away with nothing. This feels deeply unfair and frankly disrespectful of the player's time. At the very minimum, players should receive partial rewards proportional to how far they progressed in the mission.

For a game that already asks you to pay for a base game, class DLCs, and microtransactions, the least the developers could do is respect the time you actually invest in playing it.

Maybe this pc is just a bit outdated but this game is pretty unoptimized even so. I can run most games if even at medium or low settings including TW: Warhammer 3 perfectly fine with minimal lag and this game crashes a lot. Very fun game though I just wanted to own it on my PC but its too under optimized for owning right now.

You can't play singleplayer, you need a minimum of 2 people to start a private game
This will remain negative unless that changes

Unless you want to play a game made by a deaf, muted Dev team that wont interest in interact with the players community, Dont buy this game, There is no problem with Bug, But the lack of things that supposed to be in the game wasnt, for example a Solo match that most of the players deliberately request for since launch, Dev has said they will put solo mode in the game and that was 2022, 

The game did have a "private mode" but THE INGENIUS of the fatshark studio put a requirement to have "atleast 2 players" to allow you to have a private match yeah what part of their brain think this is a good idea right?

And from their experience of managing another warhammer francise before, a WH vermintide 1-2, you would have thought Darktide wouldbecome better but NO! most of the game are like in development event so the game is full released! Bot AI got alot worse than the last year, server are unstable, random game crash, the game is dead you cant find any players so you got stuck with braindead Bot teammate that has a lowest starting gear even at the high difficulty, they cant use ability or equipment like grenade and heal stim, they wont revive you when you down

It even more infuriate that i heard the Dev can simply solve alot of players complain but they chosed not to and gone silent

The only thing that kill this game is the Dev themself

Sucks that you can't play the game alone or just with bots.

You are required to be at least 2 players to play a mission.

Steam needs to add a neutral option. That said:

I like what they've done with character progression compared to VT2 but the gameplay feels flat and the lack of friendly fire makes it feel like a theme park ride we're all on together. In VT2 you had to worry about positioning and it made you feel like you were part of a team. I also don't get the hype around the melee in this game. It doesn't compete with the melee in a game like Chivalry 2, it feels like light or heavy spam with no creativity outside of the occasional unique attack.

One last thing: You can't play the game solo at all without mods, and those mods don't let you earn progress. I get it, kind of, and I know VT2 was like this as well, but sometimes I want to play solo.
far better than the game was at launch, funny horde shooter game

Visually, it looks alright. Everything on high.
Story, it's there.
Combat, smooth as butter.

But something doesn't click.

Is it the VA's? Likely not as they do a banging job. Is it that sometimes, people have the same voice and it sounds like your talking to yourself? Possibly. 
Could it be the sprint? Of course not, sprinting isn't inherently bad but does your team split up because everyone is rushing in a million directions? Perhaps.
Could it be the lack of solo play? After playing Vermintide, Left for dead and others in the Genre; Likely.
Could it be the cash shop? No. I have self control and will Yo Ho when I feel like it. The prices are silly but if you play most online games (which i don't) that's normal now. Helps that they don't look good though.
Could it be the character design? Not sure as you will wear a mask 99% of the time hiding your ugly mug.

Maybe all these little things add up to put me off from really liking this game. I bought it on sale so it's not a huge waste. although thinking about it, I would not pay a fraction of the price for this as you don't own this game when you "purchase", if I'd known sooner.

It really does live up to the title: There are warhammers, it is set 40,000 years in the future, and there is often a tide of enemies with dark intentions. If you're not good with similies or metaphors and you like titles to be even more literal than that, I would suggest you avoid this game as it will induce an autistic meltdown.

I believe that horde shooters are one of the best genres of videogame in existence and Darktide is simply another piece of evidence proving my point. The sound design is always outstanding. In combat each hit sounds meaty and feels incredibly satisfying. The music is always intense and the voice actors are all perfect and help in bringing each character to life. Speaking of the voice actors, all the dialogue is immaculate. I know I'm probably glazing them too much but all the dialogue and VOs are so incredible that I could watch a ten hour compilation of Darktide conversations and never get bored. Furthermore the gameplay itself is super fun. All the classes are a joy to experience and are relatively well balanced until the higher difficulties. I do have a few criticisms with the game (mainly with a few mechanics and enemies that I think aren't particularly fun to play against) however I these issues do not ruin my experience enough to make me hate this game. Also according to some people the game has issues with lagging however I personally have not experienced this so maybe it's something to do with how powerful your computer is? Idk. Either way this game is great. Hopefully the skitarii dlc is just as good.

The game is amazing, the combat is smooth, DLCs are really good but it is buggy. I would recommend this to 40k fans, people that love co-op horde shooters. You can customize your class heavily and there are many playstyles.
It’s a good game and visually beautiful, but it can be a bit repetitive.
dude i fricken love this game the combat is the best is the business and i just really enjoy jumping on darktide after work for a couple rounds of going absolute bananas

They need to put that fat shark on a weight loss and fitness program, cuz it keeps disconnecting me from its servers like so many exhausted heavybreathers.
Good game btw

Honestly one of the best horde shooters out there. In true L4d fashion, you've got your different enemy specials that you need to recognise, you've got a huge variety of classes that Fatshark has been adding in over time. Honestly if you love WH40k and love L4d, its a good game to get. Can't believe its 4 years and counting and i still play it.

One of the best horde shooter game available. 

Its most basic and strongest aspect is melee and ranged combat, which feels incredible but more importantly switching between them while in combat feels very organic and badass. 

Game loop handles lows and highs in a mission very well so you never get bored inside a mission. I usually do in other horde games. 

Only issue may be the optimization, with 2x frame generation I can hit 100-120 fps with a 5070 at 2k. But gameplay is fast and latency is felt much more than other games, I would play without fg if I could.

All and all this is one of those few games that you can actually enjoy for a long period of time.

By far one of the best FPS i played, would totally recommend it especially if you are into the «Left 4 Dead» formula.
BTW: I'm very impressed that my old laptop can run it flawlessly even in Havocs.

Undoubtedly the best Co-Op horde game out there right now. 82 Hours and Ive barely scratched the surface and found myself trying out all the fun new classes. FOR SURE grab if its on sale. Only negative is the premium currency and premium cosmetics are overpriced, but truthfully the earned ones look better anyway. Happy heretic hunting!
The game allows unlimited use of mods, and since it's PVE, that's great.

This genre of games has always been one of my go-to genres. And having it set in the 40K universe is awesome, even though I don't know any lore.
This game is one of my games of all time, however I cannot recommend it because of the following:
1) The optimization is horrible at best, constant FPS drops, latency issues, crashes, etc. Every time I come on this game I expect a crash.
2) The content they add to this game tells everyone that they don't listen to the community. The new classes are awesome and super fun but the 2 new game modes suck major ass. No one is even touching Mortis Trials and no one plays Expeditions, even on launch day no one was playing it because it sucks. 
3) The bugs are outrageous. So many enemies have been broken since launch. They either hit you way outside their radius of attack, your dodging just does not give you I-frames like it is intended to, or enemies who are supposed to telegraph their presence with unique sounds are completely silent and then end the whole run.

The pros are that the melee combat is out-of-this-world great and the unique class identity is wonderful. Most weapons are really fun to use and there is a sadly large number of weapons that are completely useless unless you have 5K hours and play perfectly. 

Everyone wanted this game to be the 40K version of Vermintide, but even without the comparison to Vermintide, this game is just not in good enough shape to be playable and enjoyable. Unless you're lucky enough to never experience a crash or random disconnection, you will not enjoy yourself, or you will be like me and intentionally suffer through 100s of hours even though the game constantly crashes just because the gameplay loop is chef's kiss.

Great horde shooter. Gameplay is fun, the visuals are great but the fast paced nature of the game will distract you from admiring the world around you. Music deserves all the praise. However the performance is not great, there are a lot of stutters which can be annoying. Also THERE ARE NOT ENOUGH GUNS for a FPS game. The game does not give you too many reasons to play it after you complete all the levels. All in all a game that has genuine identity but needs more content in form of gun selection and customization to give you a reason to grind. 8/10.

This game has such unique combat, so many enemies on screen, and is so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ faithful to one of the coolest fictional universes out there. If you play this game every day for 6 hours, I'm sure youll start disliking it and noticing all the flaws, which is why I believe it has plenty of negative reviews. It's also got some technical debt and can be buggy (I havent experienced it, but I did when it first launched). But if you play this game casually, a few hours every once in a while, maybe playing 10 hours a month or 20 hours a month? You will have an absolute blast if you like PvE co op shooters or Warhammer or Vermintide 2. I cannot think of a SINGLE co op game where you feel as powerful as you do in this game. Even in Space Marine 2, you genuinely feel more powerful as a character in this game. Nothing hits my dopamine receptors quite like hitting a Boom Bringer shot and seeing 10 notifications for kills on the top left of the screen instantly pop up telling everyone I just killed 10 dreg shotgunners. 

Also it's absolutely gorgeous to look at. And it has *genuine* dark levels. Many games are too afraid to make levels dark. Hell Sea of Thieves you can be in the middle of the ocean on a boat at night and you can still see completely fine, way brighter than even a full moon. This game has levels so dark it's actually hard to see and I like that (it's a modifier and there are flashlights, so if you dont like it you can just avoid that modifier).
Love it all i gotta say fun never dry very intense and extremely enjoyable <3

This is one of the worst running games i have ever played, i have never had this much of a terrible laggy mess that takes ages to load. also the bosses suck as their isnt many, Vermintide is just a superior game
Very fun squad based combat - beware the bots, though, they aren't great.

Its pretty cool and next to Vermintide it feels on par with the fluid combat and large selection of weapons.

I'm having trouble understanding the story but I did find a channel diving into the lore and I have to say this is very immersive into 40k.

Its nice to play as a Guardsman or Psyker and see things from a different perspective since I play a lot of Space Marine 2.

Pros: it's a 40k game

Cons: all of the maps look the same and once a year devs will release a new game mode that nobody plays; community is way worse than in other coop games (it's not uncommon to see dudes run away for no reason and get downed by a special and also they don't really communicate) and also the netcode/technical side/whatever is the proper term isn't very good so prepare for crashes or disconnects for no apparent reason

Also the core design of the game is flawed - rat games had a finished storyline with unique missions and hats that you could grind through cheevos - this game not only doesn't have a finished interesting (as much as genre allows) storyline but also has most of the customization option behind a paywall (wwhich is quite costly) - without irl money you can only get reskins of basic things you can get for levelling up or you can grind all cheevos for all the classes (which takes a lot of time) and get something which isn't a reskin of the basic armor. Choice of weapons andd their customization are very weirdd - apparently admech don't know how to fit both a flashlight and a bayonet and a reflex sight on a gun nor are your dudes provided with heavy weapons despite upper difficulties spawning endless waves of enemies because devs can't play on a difficulty harder than malice  and don't know how balance a game that they have made for a 3rd time now

Constant issues with servers.
I can open ingame shop without any issues, but cannot play. It feels like a joke.

Warhammer 40,000: Darktide is one of the most immersive co-op shooters available today, successfully capturing the grimdark atmosphere of the Warhammer 40K universe while delivering intense, satisfying gameplay.

In a way, it reminds me of Call of Duty Zombie Mode from a gameplay point of view, with some elements from Left 4 Dead. It's one of the most satisfying horde shooters I have ever played. The frantic gameplay, the overwhelming enemy waves, the constant pressure, the unfairness of the situation.

One of the coolest features of the game is that you can pick from 6 [i](soon 7)[/i] classes, each bringing unique abilities and playstyles to the battlefield, but you also make your own story, you can select your character's: home planet, childhood, what changed in his life, what job he had before, etc. And all this backstory affects his dialogue with other characters. So you homebrew your own story. The classes are:
[b]1. Veteran:[/b] The regular soldier ranged specialist, excelling at eliminating priority targets and supporting the team from a distance.
[b]2. Zealot:[/b] The aggressive frontline fighter who thrives in close-quarters combat and can survive situations that would overwhelm other classes.
[b]3. Psyker:[/b] A magic operative capable of devastating enemies with psychic attacks having to balance it with the risks of his own corruption.
[b]4. Ogryn:[/b] A Hulk-like human mutant, massive tank who can absorb punishment, protecting allies through sheer size and strength, and smash through enemy lines thus controlling crowds.
[b]5. Arbitrator (DLC):[/b] La policia, a law-enforcer officer equipped with riot-control gear, shields, shock weapons, and a Cyber-dog companion that fights alongside you.
[b]6. Hive Scum (DLC):[/b] The criminal underworld counterpart, a high-risk high-reward glass-cannon gunslinger, fast and aggressive but fragile using combat stimulants.
[b]7. Skitari (DLC):[/b] A cybernetic warrior of the Mechanicus, that will be released next month.

There are about 20 campaign missions, following your Reject's rise from expendable prisoner to trusted operative of the Inquisition by proving his loyality and skill. 

But replayability is a major focus, aside from the campaign you also have.
- Mission Board: randomized missions with difficulty modifiers, changing mission conditions, changed objectives, randomized enemy spawns.
- Mortis Trials: survival mode basically fight through increasingly difficult enemy waves while holding the ground. Darktide's closest equivalent to a traditional horde mode or Call of Duty Zombies-style survival experience.
- Auric & Maelstrom: increased challenge modes. Auric is an extra hard difficutly. Maelstrom increases it via "mutated" missions with weird combinations like endless specialists, hunting packs or reduced visibility.
- Expeditions: You go outside the Hive Tertium for the first time, there are semi-open maps where squads search for Tech-Remnants, explore randomized zones, decide when to extract, and balance risk versus reward. I like to call this [i]"Lethal Company in Darktide"[/i], a bit Rogue-like.
- More campaign missions in progress. The first campaign "Battle for Tertium" has 17 missions, the 2nd campaign "No Man's Land" has 3 missions but new missions are still being made.
- Havoc: Endgame challenge mode. For max level players only. With huge rewards.

[b]TL;DR:[/b]
For story: Campaign.
For wave survival: Mortis Trials.
For random maps: Mission Board.
For extra challenge: Auric + Maelstrom.
For open-world loot-focused runs: Expeditions.
For hardcore endgame: Havoc.

So you have plenty of content.

Darktide has an amazing atmosphere. Few games make you feel like you're living inside the Warhammer 40K universe. Hive Tertium is a sprawling industrial nightmare of gothic cathedrals, polluted manufactorums, endless machinery, and colossal architecture. Every mission feels like you're a tiny reject cog in a galaxy-spanning war machine. The environmental design, voice acting, and soundtrack do an incredible job of selling the setting.

The combat deserves its own paragraph because it's arguably the game's strongest feature. Darktide combines ranged shooter mechanics with some of the best melee combat in the genre. Weapons feel like they have impact and real weight behind them. A bolter fires explosive rounds that rip apart entire groups of enemies. A thunder hammer feels like you're swinging a truck into a crowd. The game constantly forces you to switch between ranged and melee combat, which keeps encounters dynamic.

[b]Negatives:[/b]
- The crafting and item progression can still feel grindy. But this has no impact on you if you only play for fun on a low to medium difficutly rather than min-maxing every build as fast as possible.
- 80% of the maps look the same: big city. This makes sense because the whole story + game modes happen in Hive Tertium, and the industrial gothic city aesthetic itself is great, but after many hours it becomes a very repetitive biome. The only real break is the other 10% is the Expeditions maps outside the city.
- New players may feel overwhelmed by the sheer number of overlapping systems and currencies. Between classes, game modes, weapon stats, perks, blessings, rarity tiers, crafting materials, and vendors, the onboarding experience can be confusing. I specifically listed here all game modes and classes because it was confusing for me. I didn't even mention: perks, weapons rarity, shops, and such.
- No true solo mode. It's public games or private games of at least 2 players. I have no idea why they went for this artificial restriction.
- No scoreboard with kills, assists, etc at the end of a level.

For currency there is:
1. Credits = buy and manage gear.
2. Plasteel = common gear upgrade material.
3. Diamantine = rare gear upgrade material.
4. Aquilas = real-money for cosmetics only.

[b]And a fake Negative:[/b]
The game has [i]"Mixed"[/i] review recently. If you look at the people who gave it negative review 20% mention performance issues which I haven't noticed any but fair. While 80% claim [i]"Higher difficulty modes are too difficult"[/i].

Yes, you heard that right. In a game with 5 difficulty levels, from [i]"anything works, have a chill time bro"[/i] to [i]"you must play perfect"[/i], they complain that the hardest difficulty is too difficult.

And they see nothing dumb with that complaint.

Why not switch to a lower difficulty level? answer: ego. I have a friend who managed to beat every single mission in the game on the highest difficulties, Auric and Havoc. I have also seen him do it flawlessly. Now that's a real tryhard.

The people who complain that [i]"the highest difficulty in the game is too difficulty"[/i] suffer from a combination of skill issues & gamer pride. The world will end if they select a lower difficulty even though they are clearly not good enough for the highest difficulty.

Being tryhard & failing is not a good reason to give a dislike. If the game had only 1 difficulty option they may have had a point, but the game has 5, and you're just upset you're not good enough for max difficulty.

I don't care if I offend them, they offended the game first, you can dislike a game for a good reason or a dumb reason. And [i]"I have skill issues at the highest difficulty but am too prideful to play on a lower difficulty"[/i] is one of the dumbest reasons.

Overall, 8.5/10. My main issues are 80% of the maps have the same visual repetition. I wished there were some maps with snow, forest, swamps, outside of a ship, I don't know what excuse they could have added in the story for this. And too many systems although I can see how other players love too many systems. Other than that, great Call of Duty Zombie Mode-like feel, great co-op, works for both casual players on lower difficulties and tryhards with perfect build, great numerous game modes, nice class balance, love that you can make your own story for your own characters. Great game.

I played Darktide a year after release in 2023, and revisiting this in 2026 I was suprised how little they added over the past 4 years. Basically 2 classes and a skill-tree rework - That seems to be the sum of it.

Progression systems (talents, masteries, itemization) basically end at level 30. So they're about as deep as a puddle and honestly just feel kinda obsolete? All you're really left with is replaying the same 5 zones or so on higher and higher difficulties. 

I really enjoy the higher difficulty of the game and its combat - Its satisfying and as has lot depth to it. Unfortunately it just gets old kinda quickly, replaying the same thing over and over again with more/harder monsters, but no lanes of progression other than difficulty. Plus the amount of content delivered for a live service is absolutely pathetic. 

If this were free to play, I would recommend it - But its not, so stay away.
Returning to it after a long break, lots more to the game now. Feels finished, filled out and much more optimized than a year or two ago whenev I first played

Darktide, the game Vermintide should have been in the first place, is well worth it. There's too small off a fanbase to populate a match most hours of the day, unless you're on Russian time.  The visuals are great, the character classes are differentiated enough to make it worthwhile building several characters, and you have an actual range of enemies that require a change in tactics. Too often Vermintide ends up being a mouse hammering exercise as you use nothing but your primary weapon to knock aside rats in pinch points until you can scamper in a panic to the next point it is possible to hold.

I could do without the pointless cosmetic monetization, especeially since you can't see your own damn character outside of the matchmaking lobby. I don't particularly care what kind of hat I'm wearing when the action is so frantic I am paying more attention to the tag above a characters head than the bandoleer on their shirt. I guess if you have money to burn it's nice to have the optioin to play dressup, which I admittedly did a lot of in Mordheim, but if you don't have the spare capital it has little effect.

I particularly love the range of weapons in this title vs the other squad shooters in the Warhammer 40k universe. There's enough different weapons, both melee (a bit underpowered for the 40k franchise) and ranged (don't bother with the autopistols), that you can customize your build to suit every possible FPS approach. It takes a while to get to the point you can use a bolter, and honestly if you are going to buy any DLC get the Arbites class. The cybermastiff is the best addition to any class, covering your six while you have to ♥♥♥♥ around with cogitators and the satisfying range of puzzle games required to operate them.

Some of the mechanics have obvisouly been designed with a controller in mind, but if you want to be able to shoot with any accuracy you need a decent keyboard and mouse setup. I've never seen the point in playing an FPS with a twin stick if you have the option of a mouse; the more precise accuracy of the mouse controls make for a proper shooting experience. Having to swing the weapon around at a limited range of speeds is less realistic than being able to pick the mouse up and swipe quickly to perform a 180 spin and catch things behind you. It also makes it easier to do the spin-jump that lets you leap off a ledge and land facing backwards to shoot the hoard of nurgle zombies pouring over the fance behind you.

This is the best online shooter in the 40k collection I've played yet. I'm not that far in but I can see many hundreds of hours of play getting absorbed in the near future. It's addictive to the point of absurdity, the game loop doles out rewards quickly enough that it doesn't become a slog the way Vermintide did.

If you want to slaughter a billion silly ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ in the ruins of the underhives with big guns that go THUMP and improvised clubs that mash heads to goop this is your best choice. I'm hoping the upcoming class expansion proves worth it, but in the meantime the available content is enough to keep you wasting hours in the depths of the hive worlds for years to come.

I do have one serious complaint with Darktide, and that's some of the throw away commentary by the swarms of enemies. I was playing one night and heard one of the running targets utter a rape threat, which honestly is utterly inexcusable. Who the ♥♥♥♥ writes in rape threats to a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ game? Like what the absolute utter ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ ♥♥♥♥ is that about? This is the kind of despicable nonsense that encourages the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ hoardes of mysogyinist pricks that flock to the 40k franchise to be the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ they are. This kind of thing needs to be addressed, especially since they bothered to have a profanity filter in the text chat. Do better.

Needs some solo / bot mode, as a first time player you have quite a hard time to find any party at all.. And not everyone want's to use ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Discord to maybe find a few people.

Otherwise too much micro transactions for a paid game which also get's new DLCs every few months..

There's a reason this game is one of my overall favourites, there are very few horde shooters that best it - The design of every map and even just the hub are stunning and every class feels great to play with multiple different weapons and specs each (obviously if you take the time to actually build them, and yes I know overall psyker and hive scum are noticeably better than other classes because of their AOE potential), I'm practically 600 hours deep and still heavily enjoying every run I do.

Now that being said, Fatshark have gotten greedier and greedier as time has gone on.. most major updates to the game these days come in the form of recoloured FOMO skins, paid DLC classes (none of which get any customisation outside of the skin they're released with??) or game modes with no real reason to play it outside of Penances, they've near enough completely stopped listening to what the player base want (what the hell happened to solo mode?);  the game is absolutely incredible but it's painfully obvious that it'll never get the same love Vermintide 2 gets. 
If you can ignore the fact the devs seem to have gotten lazy and just play the game for the love of the game then I can highly recommend it, you can easily blast out 100+ hours of fun with ease.

While the game does have a few issues; dodges not always proccing as you would like, enemies that feel too quiet. It is really fun. Also the prices for in game cosmetics using IRL money/currency feels rather fair and balanced compared to other games. I do wish that some things were easier to do when solo at higher levels (in cases where bots fill your team if no other players are available) but that's probably a skill issue on my part. My favorite thing so far is weapon mastery is shared across characters provided they can use that weapon as well. Glory to the beneficent emperor!
Got the game on a promo like a year and half ago but just started to play now and wow. Cool stuff, crazy fights  and of course its warhammer universe ;)  10/10

It's frustrating try to get a semi-constant 60fps with every setting toggled to off or low (on hardware that is above the recommended system requirements). In any mission you do, the frame drops and stuttering are constant. I use AMD so maybe that's why?

I've even followed guides like these to try to optimize the game in any way possible, but I still get the stuttering and fps drops. This was mainly through changing the .ini files and using mods with Nexus Mod Manager.

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3626143482
https://github.com/thyazide/Darktide-Performance-Optimizations

I'm not sure why this game runs horribly, and I really hope it improves.

Darktide is a great game, but Poxbursters and Trappers are incredibly frustrating, especially because of their bugs. Audio cues often fail to play, leading to silent Poxbursters and Trappers attacking with little or no warning. Combined with Trapper nets having inconsistent hitboxes that can catch players even when they appear to miss. This is especially problematic considering in high-level play, a single down or death can easily domino into a loss. I've had Poxbursters fall towards me when I shoved them instead of away.

Devs, fix your game. While you're at it, nerf Trapper.

Edit: While you're at it, can we remove health chip damage despite having toughness? Always frustrating to see my health bar go down even though my toughness never broke. Especially sickening as a Martyr Zealot.

Yes there are bugs, no it isn't optimized the best(still entirely playable, locked at 60 on medium with my setup) yes youre going to get mad occasionally. But this is the best horde shooter Ive ever come across and have yet to come accross. Dont overthink this game at all, jump in, make your character and go killing. You get better as you progress into the higher difficulties, thats where the true fun to this game starts.

Based on its own merits, and without comparing it to other titles in the horde genre, Darktide is a very fun and solid game.

*PROS*:
-The art direction is amazing. Characters and environments are packed with detail, and players who enjoy environmental storytelling will find plenty to appreciate.

-Character customization. There is a little something for everyone, from hardened veterans to stalwart protectors to pious zealots and cutthroats - you get the picture. Picking different backgrounds and personalities also changes your interactions with your squadmates. This adds a lot of flavour to the world and really helps make it feel more alive.

-The cosmetics are generally high quality, though the pricings for premium pieces may not appeal to everyone.

-The campaign does a great job of introducing WH40K lore in a way that's accessible to newcomers. Missions flow well together, and the variety of maps, enemies, and game modes (campaign, extraction, roguelite, etc.) provides plenty of replay value.

*CONS*:
-Some classes could use balance adjustments or more interesting skill trees to make them shine on higher difficulties.

-I'd like to see more content added to the game. While there is a solid amount of options right now, additional enemies and maps would go a long way to keeping things fresh. Plus, some classes lack weapon variety compared to others (looking at you big guy), so there's that.

*VERDICT*:
Overall, I'm having a great time with the game. It's one of the best horde games at the moment imho, and if you're interested in WH40K, I highly recommend giving it a try. Also thank you devs for supporting this game throughout the years; as a fairly new player, I love the fact that there are still things I can look forward to. Keep it up!
Loved this game. If you know any similar titles, feel free to drop your recommendations in the comments!

If you like PvE games that are similar to Left 4 Dead and Vermintide then this is a good game to get.

Fun, polished, and atmospheric, but too many issues to recommend. There's tons of unavoidable spam-clicking, so pray for your wrists. It's quite short and doesn't complete the story, not that I really cared that much because the writing was among the blandest and most forgettable I've ever played through - even having just completed it I'm struggling to remember how it went in any detail.  Difficulty curve is a little wonky if you go up when prompted - one step up is much bigger than the previous 3 and best saved for lvl 30. Dialogue between the party members (Which is extremely weird as a concept, incidentally - why is the game roleplaying with our characters, and why is my guy such a ♥♥♥♥?) runs out very quickly and begins to repeat even doing each mission once a piece.

Mostly, though, that last part is my real issue - everything has the feel of bracing itself for an endgame that consists of repeating the same missions you've already done with mutators. You've gutted what I would define as the Main Game - a singular completion of each mission in the story order at suggested difficulty - in order to facilitate something that seems more like a throw-in timewaster for the very dedicated.

It plays like a common arcade free to play shooter except that it's not free... There doesn't seem to be any content outside shooting things in various environments and leveling skill trees to shoot more things. It's very repetitive in that way.

There seems to be a lot of care and money put into the art and cutscenes but the story is not impactful at all. So you end up just skipping all the cutscenes since you completing or failing a mission affects absolutely nothing. 

There are several currency types which doesn't make any sense, and there is very very little to spend it on...

I wish they had more of a survival game aspect in that your weapons wear out so you have to patch up and replace them from time to time. Also purchasing loadout items before missions so you actually have some sense of strategy and preparation. 

There are a couple "shops" but are extremely limited.  

But yeah for a $40 game, there is very little content.

Do you enjoy feeling a high level of stress? Does the idea of having only a few rounds left while a tsunami of horde rushes toward you appeal to you? Does solving a puzzle in a short amount of time while monsters are attacking you sound exciting? Do you enjoy battling monsters beyond man's horrific imagination where the only comfort is the banter between your character and your teammates that makes James Gunn's Suicide Squad sound vapid in comparison?

Well look no further. This game is for you. It is fast paced and hilariously fun. The gameplay is great, the story and lore is full of mystery, the combat is really epic, the weapons are devastatingly fun, and the banter between the characters makes the stress all worth it.

As many pros as there are, I do have a few cons. The lack of customization such as faces, hairstyles, beards, and tattoos is noticeable. The variety of introduced outfits is strictly for the standard classes but not for the Arbitrator or the Hive Scum, they have limited options.

Another con is the after finishing the story, the game becomes repetitive with harder modes. Great for challenges, but not for the lore hungry folks out there. I was hoping to see more story development with access to new areas in Atoma Prime. I really wish there was a way to give suggestions because I would like to see private quarters like those in Outlast Trials where they can be decorated with items you received from achievements and trophies.

That said, I am excited for the next class and hope the developers expand more of the game's story.

While I've put 100+ hours into the game, I wish steam had a middle option where its in between. Game is fun sure but its optimization is pretty abysmal at times in combat, the balancing can be bad or good depending on opinion, no solo mode which is still a highly requested topic all the time and dlc classes which imo can be on the verge of p2w.

For good combat is fun, classes even tho the dlc ones are better can still be meaningful to the mission and still carry their fun itself, the sound design music and environments is so excellent crafted, guns feel awesome to use in terms of design, feel and sound, in combat is iffy depending on the gun, also tons of content for those wanting different ways to play like havoc an increasing in diff mission with modifiers, expedition for those wanting to be off tertium and in the wasteland, main missions for story and simple route gameplay and mortis trials for wave survival.

I feel the game is about 7/10 would recommend when its on a good sale

Came for the chainsword, stayed for the soundtrack.
2.6 hours on record and my current summary of the game is:
      Absolute industrial techno bangers blasting in my ears.
      Swinging a giant sword into a wall of infected heretics.
      Trying not to get separated from the squad and instantly pinned by a mutant.
10/10 first impressions. Will continue to serve the Inquisition

This game is very fun with friends, also solo. Most of the time you are having fun and screaming just because there can be some time where they may come out of nowhere and scare you, But in the end your just having a fun time no matter what. But this could just be me. But Highly suggest if you like horde shooters, there is also story behind it if you want to learn more about that. Of course there is some bug's but even then, they still update the game, and add more stuff. Nothing could ever be the best but this it still not a bad game.
9/10 game

This is just straight up a much better game than Space Marine 2. The weapons feel different and incredibly punchy (bolt pistol is amazing) and the melee is insanely satisfying. Generally seeing the weapons in such detail from a first person perspective is incredibly and you really feel the power of higher end kit as a measly guardsmen.

Darktide and Space Marine 2 are both horde type games however what distinguishes this from SM2 (and why I call SM2 a "Horde Masher" is this gives you the ability to build your character with large skill trees that can completely change how each class plays. You also have the added layer of weapons that each have their own perks and "blessings" which further give depth to the build options.

The gameplay is far above that of SM2 where you essentially replay the same level except in 8 different colors for your character that you pay 20 dollars for "waaaaaaoooooww space marine different color" while you mash hordes and eat crayons.

Best Weapon feeling on the market. Distinct classes, great fighting and tactical situations in endgame. One of the best coop shooters!

5/5
